question:
Will the Blast bridge be exploited by end of February?

Description: This is a market on whether the Blast bridge suffers an exploit between November 24, 2023 and February 28, 2024, 11:59 PM ET. 

The resolution source for this market will be the Rekt News leaderboard, currently available at https://rekt.news/leaderboard/.

If the Blast bridge is listed on the resolution source as a protocol having suffered an exploit before the resolution date, regardless of the size or ranking, this market will resolve to “Yes”. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

Only exploits which have a date listed on the Rekt News Leaderboard between November 24, 2023 and February 28, 2024, (inclusive) will count.

In the case there is ambiguity over the validity or availability of Rekt's Leaderboard, the project's primary communication channels (e.g. Twitter), and other credible crypto publications will be referenced.

This market will resolve as soon as the protocol is listed on the resolution source.

evidences.title: Blast network reduces bridge process to 7 days, cutting withdrawal time in half
evidences.content: Blast announced the update in a social media post on July 16 and explained that the reduction in bridge time comes after a thorough analysis of withdrawal activity over the past four months. The analysis showed core contributors that a smaller buffer would still accommodate nearly all withdrawal requests. Previously, the 14-day bridging period was set to provide a buffer for Lido withdrawals, which Blast currently relies on for Ethereum yield. By reducing this period, Blast aims to enhance the efficiency and user experience on its platform. [...] ## Layer-2 classification The network's X, formerly Twitter, account recently removed mentions of “Layer-2” from its handle and details, prompting some to speculate that the rollup plans to become a standalone network. ### Daily signals, zero noise. Market-moving headlines and context delivered every morning in one tight read. Free. No spam. Unsubscribe any time. However, Blast founder Tieshun Roquerre stated in a video that the network's current implementation is as a Layer-2, but this could change in the future if it benefits the user and adds value. He added that being an Ethereum Layer-2 is “an implementation detail” and that “it do...

evidences.title: Using Blast Bridge | Blast Docs
evidences.content: # Using Blast Bridge How to deposit and withdraw assets using official Blast Bridge You can use the Testnet bridge to get acquainted with the UI of the official Blast bridge. The official Blast Bridge on Mainnet operates in exactly the same way connecting to Ethereum Mainnet. ## Deposit ETH from L1 to Blast Follow the steps below to deposit your tokens from Goerli to Blast Testnet: Navigate to the testnet bridge and connect your wallet by clicking on the button in the top-right. Select the "Deposit" tab and enter the amount of $ETH you want to bridge over to Blast Testnet, Enter an amount to deposit and then click on the Deposit button at the bottom to proceed with token approval. Confirm your transaction on the Metamask [...] Patience is key. Allow the system some time to confirm the status of your withdrawal. Perform proof to confirm the withdrawal After the withdrawal status is confirmed, proceed to perform the necessary proof to validate the withdrawal. Wait for the challenge period and execute a claim to receive your ETH on L1 Be patient during the challenge period. Once it concludes, execute the claim process to receive your ETH on the Layer 1 (L1) network. Feel free to ad...

evidences.title: Bridge Security Guidelines, 2nd Edition - TRID
evidences.content: # Bridge Security Guidelines, 2nd Edition These guidelines offer guidance on bridge design for human-induced extreme events. They provide information on the response of concrete bridge columns subjected to blast loads, as well as blast-resistant design and detailing guidelines and analytical models of blast load distribution. The guidelines include discussion on how to potentially reduce risk to other structural bridge components (e.g., bridge towers, cable stays, suspender ropes), as well as other intentional hazards to consider for threat vulnerability risk assessments (nonexplosive cutting devices, collisions or impacts, and fire). This second edition includes additional resources for identifying potential solutions to mitigate risk from other intentional hazards. evidences.title: Cross-Chain Bridge Security Audits | SigIntZero
evidences.content: Skip to content # Cross-Chain Bridge Security Audits Bridges are the highest-risk infrastructure in crypto. They combine multi-chain logic, off-chain relayers, consensus validation, and massive asset custody into a single attack surface. Bridge exploits account for over $3 billion in losses, more than any other protocol category. Request an Audit ## Why Bridges Are the Hardest Audit Target Cross-chain bridges are architecturally unique. They span multiple blockchains, rely on off-chain infrastructure, and custody assets from every chain they connect. A vulnerability in any layer (smart contracts, relayer logic, validator sets, or message verification) can drain every asset the bridge holds. evidences.title: Importance of Blockchain Security & Auditing in Web3 Bridges
evidences.content: In crypto, cross-chain bridges play a crucial role in enhancing interoperability by connecting various blockchains. They enable users to seamlessly transfer their cryptocurrencies or tokens from one blockchain to another. Unfortunately, they are among the most high risk infrastructure in the blockchain ecosystem. If you are building a bridge, request a Bridge audit today. ## Cross-Chain Bridge Use Cases Cross-chain bridges are protocols that let users port their cryptocurrency from one blockchain to another, usually by locking the user’s assets into a smart contract on the original chain, and then minting equivalent assets on the second chain. [...] A user who owns BTC may want to interact with protocols or DApps on the Ethereum network to do this they may sell their BTC and buy ETH but this will expose them to price volatility and transaction fees. Bridges solves this problem, the user can simply use a cross-chain bridge and send their BTC to get WBTC( Wrapped Bitcoin), an ERC20 token with the same price as BTC that can be used on the Ethereum blockchain. Apart from tokens, cross-chain bridges can be used to also transfer NFTs and data from one blockchain to another. ## ‍The Ri...

evidences.title: What is Bridge Risk? | Cube Exchange
evidences.content: And if the bridge aims for light-client verification, it shifts risk toward correctness of proof systems, client maintenance, and compatibility with each chain’s consensus model. This often produces the strongest cryptographic story, but also a narrower implementation envelope and more engineering burden. So “bridge risk” is not one thing. It is the shape taken by cross-chain failure under a particular design. ## How can you reduce bridge risk and limit an exploit's blast radius? [...] If the bridge is mainly message passing, then the risk is not only financial theft in the narrow sense. A forged message can trigger arbitrary downstream actions: releasing funds from another protocol, updating governance state, executing a cross-chain trade, or changing permissions. Chainlink CCIP and Wormhole both emphasize token transfer plus arbitrary messaging because that is what developers actually want. But arbitrary messaging means the blast radius depends on what destination contracts do when they receive a “valid” message. The bridge may be secure enough to move bytes correctly while the receiving application remains dangerously permissive. [...] What is Bridge Risk? hero image ## Intro...

evidences.title: Blockchain Bridge Security Comparison: Trust Models ... - Spark
evidences.content: Spark Tools/Explorers # Blockchain Bridge Security Comparison: Trust Models and Track Records Compare blockchain bridge security models, audit history, and track records: Wormhole, LayerZero, Axelar, and more. Spark TeamInvalid Date ## Bridge Security Models Compared Cross-chain bridges have become critical infrastructure for moving assets between blockchains, but they remain one of the most exploited components in crypto. Since 2022, bridge hacks have accounted for over 69% of total funds stolen in DeFi, with cumulative losses exceeding $2.8 billion from bridge exploits alone. Choosing the right bridge means understanding the trust model underneath it: who validates messages, what happens when validators misbehave, and how much capital is at risk. [...] Several patterns emerge from these incidents. Compromised private keys were the attack vector in the Ronin, Harmony, and IoTeX hacks. Smart contract vulnerabilities enabled the Wormhole and Nomad exploits. Low-threshold multisigs (2-of-5, 5-of-9) were disproportionately targeted because fewer keys need to be compromised. The Lazarus Group (DPRK) was attributed to multiple attacks, with North Korean actors stealing at least $2 bi...

evidences.title: New Ethereum Layer 2 Blast Attracts $30M Hours After Bridge Goes Live
evidences.content: Blast Timeline Early Access (now) - bridge to earn yield + Blast Points Mainnet (Feb 24) - dapps go live, withdrawals enabled Redemption (May 24) - Blast Points can be redeemed Of the total funds bridged, data shows over $19 million in ether has been staked on Lido, where it is set to earn as much as 4% annualized yield. Another $3 million is on Maker, while a smaller tranche of $150,000 in dai (DAI) stablecoins sits idle in the wallet. Users who bridge stablecoins receive Blast’s auto-rebasing stablecoin, USDB. The yield for USDB comes from MakerDAO’s on-chain T-Bill protocol. Blast raised over $20 million in a round led by Paradigm and Standard Crypto and is headed by pseudonymous figurehead @PacmanBlur, one of the co-founders of NFT marketplace Blur. evidences.title: Blockchain Bridge Security Comparison: Trust Models and Track Records | Spark
evidences.content: For a comparison of bridge fees rather than security models, see thebridge fee calculator. ## Trust Model Architectures Bridge security fundamentally comes down to who can authorize a cross-chain message and what happens if they act maliciously. There are four main architectural approaches in production today. ### Multisig / Guardian Networks [...] This comparison covers six major bridge protocols: Wormhole, LayerZero (via Stargate), Axelar, Across, Hop, and their underlying security architectures. Each protocol makes different tradeoffs between decentralization, speed, and trust assumptions. [...] ### Optimistic Verification (Across, Hop) Across Protocol uses an intent-based architecture secured by UMA's Optimistic Oracle. A bonded relayer fulfills the user's transfer on the destination chain, then a dataworker submits a bundle of fulfilled intents to UMA for verification. A one-hour challenge window opens: if no one disputes the claim, it is accepted. If challenged, UMA's Data Verification Mechanism resolves the dispute through token-holder voting.

Choose the route, review the live quote, and sign from your own wallet. Open App How it works Non-custodial Blast-ready routing Wallet-native swaps No sign-up Bridge Preview From Amount in app To Live quote in app Route, timing and network fees are shown before you sign. Open App Preview only · non-custodial · connect your wallet in the app How it works ## Bridge Blast in three steps The flow is built for Blast users who want a direct DeFi route: connect a wallet, pick the asset path, then approve only after the app shows the live details. 1 ### Connect your wallet [...] 1 ### Connect your wallet Open the app and connect a Web3 wallet. Your keys stay with you, and every transfer starts with your signature. 2 ### Choose the Blast route Select ETH, BLAST or another supported asset path into the Layer 2 network. The app keeps the route readable before you continue. 3 ### Review and confirm Check the destination, quote, expected time and route costs, then confirm from your wallet when the transaction matches your intent. evidences.title: Bridge To and From Blast - Rubic Exchange
evidences.content: Rubic logo en # Bridge To and From Blast Decorative cube Decorative cube ## What Is Blast? Blast is a Web3 infrastructure and decentralized RPC provider that supports multiple blockchains, enhancing dApp performance by offering fast and reliable connections to blockchain networks. Blast provides developers with tools to access blockchain data and execute transactions, enabling better scalability and higher efficiency for dApps. Blast is a Web3 infrastructure and decentralized RPC provider that supports multiple blockchains, enhancing dApp performance by offering fast and reliable connections to blockchain networks. ## How to Bridge To Blast? ## Supported Networks [...] ## How to Bridge To Blast? ## Supported Networks Arbitrum Optimism Near Tron Bitcoin Polygon zkEVM TON Base Linea zkSync Era Scroll Mantle Solana Blast Avalanche Taiko Polygon Kroma ## Supported Tokens Ethereum Blast Tiny Tiger Cat Renzo ETH Wrapped ETH USD Coin Andy BlastCat USDB Wrapped BTC Blast INU Axelar ## Popular Bridges ## FAQs About Bridging To and From Blast ### What is a crypto bridge? ### Why should I use Blast bridge on Rubic? ### How long does it take to get through the Blast bridge on Rubic? ### Is...

evidences.title: terminology - What is the security purpose of a bridge? - Network Engineering Stack Exchange
evidences.content: leeand00's user avatar ## 4 Answers 4 The bridge / switch concept wasn't created as a security solution. Its main goal was to alleviate the problem that arises when there is a high number of devices attached to a single ethernet network. When ethernet networks were a bunch of hubs together you could have hundreds of workstations sharing the same collision domain and the same broadcast domain. As more stations you have in a single collision domain, the chances of transmitting without collisions is lower, and the results are: more retransmissions and a perception of slow network. A bridge divides the network in a number of collision domains equal to the number of ports the bridge has and maintaining a single broadcast domain. [...] However the bridge support in linux is more than just a bridge. Bridge traffic passes through ebtables and by default IP traffic on a bridge passes through iptables (this can be disabled). This allows one to implement packet filtering and can therefore allow a firewall to be introduced without breaking up the layer-2 domain. Peter Green's user avatar Packets are layer 3 items. Frames are layer 2 items. Routers route packets and bridges/switches pass fra...

evidences.title: Repair the bridge before it cracks: Understanding vulnerabilities and weaknesses in modern IT
evidences.content: ### Bridge the gaps before they become failures Weaknesses may not always demand immediate attention like CVEs, but addressing them now prevents future security crises and is key for a Secure by Design approach to product development. By shifting our perspective and making weakness resolution a standard part of secure development, we can reduce vulnerabilities, strengthen security, and create more resilient software systems. Just as maintaining a bridge provides a safer, smoother journey, proactive security helps deliver a safer, more resilient product. Press release ## Red Hat Security Solutions Arm Enterprise Organizations ### About the authors Austin Kimbrell, Senior Product Security Engineer, Red Hat ### Austin Kimbrell Senior Product Security Engineer [...] ## Finding and resolving weaknesses At Red Hat, we identify weaknesses through a structured secure development lifecycle (SDLC). We analyze patterns, behaviors, and common flaws, and integrate this information into best practices and guidelines (as we did, for example, with Kubernetes Operators: good security practices.) Just like structural engineers use preventative maintenance to keep bridges safe, we use these insigh...
